Project/Plant Name: Ponale (Riva del Garda Ledro) Hydroelectric Power Station
Description/Notes: Ponale Hydroelectric Power Plant is a pumped storage plant, located in city of Riva del Garda in the Italian province of Trentino, on River Ponale between Lake Ledro and Lake Garda. The station was built in 1928-1929 and underwent overhaul in 1998. The station is known by the world record in high-rise concrete pumping during the reconstruction in 1994. The achieved record was 532m.

Ponale Hydroelectric Power Plant is a pumped storage plant, located in city of Riva del Garda in the Italian province of Trentino, on River Ponale between Lake Ledro and Lake Garda. The station was built in 1928-1929 and underwent overhaul in 1998. The station is known by the world record in high-rise concrete pumping during the reconstruction in 1994. The achieved record was 532m.

The Folsom Dam JFP is a collaborative project aimed at increasing flood protection capacity and reducing hydrologic, seismic, and static risks. It includes the construction of a new auxiliary spillway and other infrastructure improvements.
ISO/RTO: Terna

Description/Notes: The Rantau Dedap geothermal power plant in South Sumatra, Indonesia, has a capacity of 220MW and is developed by PT Supreme Energy Rantau Dedap (SERD). It uses direct contact condensing steam technology and is part of Indonesia's second 10,000MW accelerated development programme. The project aims to provide clean electricity to approximately 490,000 households and offset 1.1 million tonnes of CO2 emissions annually.

The Jurong Island CCGT Plant is a 500MW combined-cycle power plant located in the Tembusu sector of Jurong Island, Singapore. It is operated by Keppel Merlimau Cogen (KMC) and supplies power to the Singaporean network. The plant uses imported natural gas with an auto-switchover to diesel standby firing. It features Alstom's GT13E2 gas turbines and provides utilities like steam, firefighting water, and cooling water to consumers on Jurong Island.
ISO/RTO: Energy Market Authority (EMA)

Description/Notes: The BEE Ghent Biomass Power Plant, located at the Port of Ghent, is the largest biomass-fired power plant in Belgium. It produces 215MW of electricity and 110MW of thermal energy using woodchips, wood pellets, and agro-residues. The Alamo Solar Power Project is a 400MW solar photovoltaic power plant located in San Antonio, Texas. Developed through a public-private partnership between OCI Solar Power and CPS Energy, the project consists of seven individual PV power stations constructed in multiple phases. The project generates enough electricity to power approximately 70,000 households and contributes significantly to the local economy. It includes notable phases such as Alamo 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7, with various contractors and suppliers involved in its development.
ISO/RTO: ERCOT

The prototype plant has a designed capacity to generate 10kW of electricity and is mainly used for testing and development purposes.

Description/Notes: The Calvados Offshore Wind Farm, also known as Courseulles-sur-Mer, is a 448MW project located in the English Channel, off the coast of Northern France. It is being developed by a consortium including EDF Renewables, Enbridge's EIH, and wpd. The project is expected to generate clean energy for 630,000 people and is scheduled for commissioning in 2024. It includes 64 Siemens Gamesa SWT-7.0-154 turbines and involves significant infrastructure for grid connection. Description/Notes: Hornsea Project One is the world's largest offshore wind farm, located off the Yorkshire coast, UK. It has a capacity of 1.2GW and started operations in June 2019. The project is a joint venture between Orsted and Global Infrastructure Partners, and it powers approximately one million homes in the UK.

The Nelson Dewey 3 project was a proposed 300MW coal/biomass plant in Cassville, Wisconsin, intended to upgrade the existing Nelson Dewey plant. The project was rejected by state regulators due to high costs compared to alternatives. It aimed to use circulating fluidised bed technology to burn coal and biomass, including switchgrass, corn stover, and waste wood. The project was intended to open in 2013 but was never built.

Description/Notes: Lodi Energy Center is a 300 MW combined-cycle natural gas power plant in California owned and operated by the Northern California Power Agency (NCPA). Construction began in July 2010 and the plant entered service in August 2012. It uses Siemens Flex Plant 30 technology and the SPPAÃƒÆ’Ã‚Â¢ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¡Ã‚Â¬ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â€šÂ¬Ã‹Å“T3000 control system for fast start-up and high efficiency (up to ~57%). Project/Plant Name: Shuweihat S3 Combined Cycle Power Plant
Description/Notes: 1,600 MW gas-fired combined cycle plant at Ruwais, Abu Dhabi (UAE). Developed by Shuweihat Asia Power Company (SAPCO): ADWEA 60%, Sumitomo & KEPCO 40%. Construction began 2011; first gas turbine ignition July 2013; commercial operation August 2014. Two CCGT blocks: two Siemens SGT5-4000F gas turbines, one SST5-4000 steam turbine, and two dual-pressure HRSGs with bypass stacks. Air-cooled generators for GTs, hydrogen-cooled generator for ST; seawater-cooled condensers. Description/Notes: Run-of-the-river hydroelectric plant on the Wangchu River in Chukha Dzongkhag, Bhutan. Total installed capacity 1,020 MW via six 170 MW generators in an underground powerhouse. Generates ~4,865 GWh/year. Includes a 92 m high dam and a 22 km headrace tunnel; three 440 kV transmission lines export power to India. Commissioning delayed from 2005 to March 2007; plant dedicated May 2008. Construction faced severe geological/tunneling challenges. Funded by the Government of India (60% grant, 40% loan). Transmission to northern India developed via PPP (Power Grid Corporation of India and Tata Power) with BOOT lines operated for 30 years.
ISO/RTO: None (Bhutan/India do not operate ISO/RTO markets; dispatch via national/regional load dispatch centres)
System Operator: Tala-Delhi Transmission (JV of Power Grid Corporation of India and Tata Power) for export lines; Tala Hydroelectric Project Authority for plant operations
Energy Storage Technology Provider: Not applicable (hydropower project); major equipment supplier: Bharat Heavy Electricals Limited (BHEL)
Installer: Hindustan Construction Company (C1, C4); Larsen & Toubro (C3); Jaiprakash Industries (C2, C5)
Ownership Model (Other): Government-to-government joint project; managed by Tala Hydroelectric Project Authority (THPA), funded by Government of India (60% grant, 40% loan at 9%).
Owner(s): Tala Hydroelectric Project Authority (THPA); Governments of Bhutan and India (joint project)

Description/Notes: A 9 GW HVDC project developed by ENOWA to connect Saudi ArabiaÃƒÆ’Ã‚Â¢ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¡Ã‚Â¬ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¾Ã‚Â¢s Yanbu region to NEOMÃƒÆ’Ã‚Â¢ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¡Ã‚Â¬ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¾Ã‚Â¢s industrial city (NIC/Oxagon). The first link (YanbuÃƒÆ’Ã‚Â¢ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¡Ã‚Â¬ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â€šÂ¬Ã…â€œNIC) is 207 km with ~450 towers and two HVDC Light (525 kV) converter stations in a bipole configuration (two asymmetrical monopoles), each station rated 1.5 GW and connected to 380 kV AC grids. Phase 1 completion is targeted for July 2027 with an estimated $145M investment. Phase 2 adds two 525 kV converter stations at Yanbu and NEOM South plus associated DC overhead lines. Contractors: Hitachi Energy (HVDC design/technology/commissioning), SSEM (AC equipment, construction/install), Saudi Electricity Company (EPCM partner sourcing converters), Larsen & Toubro (525 kV link construction), Hyundai E&C (EPC), CESI (scope/technical support for phase 2).
ISO/RTO: N/A (Saudi Arabia has no ISO/RTO; TSO is National Grid SA)
System Operator: ENOWA (NEOM utility) with National Grid SA (SEC) for transmission operations
Installer: Saudi Services for Electro Mechanic Works (SSEM)

Description/Notes: Thermal power plant in Ghazlan, Saudi Arabia, adding 2,400 MW via four 600 MW dual oil/gas-fired steam turbine units to an existing 1,600 MW site. Built for Sceco-East; first unit online in 1999, construction completed March 2001 followed by commissioning. EPC by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries and Bechtel; project cost about $1.7bn with significant associated transmission upgrades. Financed largely by Sceco-East with a $500m 10-year loan; multiple contractors handled substations and transmission expansions.

Paired Grid Resources: Hydroelectric pumped storage between Carter Lake and Flatiron Reservoir (part of the Colorado-Big Thompson Project)
Description/Notes: Conventional hydro plant within the Colorado-Big Thompson Project at Flatiron Reservoir. Two main power units plus a reversible pump-turbine (Unit 3). Total generating capacity is 94,500 kW; Unit 3 can generate 8,500 kW when water is released from Carter Lake back to Flatiron Reservoir, and it can pump water up with a maximum lift of 297 ft (discharge up to 370 cfs into Carter Lake). In 2009, $14m ARRA funding supported penstock recoating and efficiency upgrades. Storage capacity and discharge duration not specified in source.
ISO/RTO: None (WECC region; Balancing Authority: WAPA-RM)
System Operator: U.S. Bureau of Reclamation (Colorado-Big Thompson Project); power marketed/transmitted by Western Area Power Administration (WAPA)
Developer: US Department of the Interior Bureau of Reclamation
Owner(s): US Department of the Interior, Bureau of Reclamation
Funding Source 1: American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) stimulus via US Bureau of Reclamation

Power Conversion System.Topology: Reversible pump-turbine (Francis-type) coupled to motor-generator; conventional pump-generator operation

Paired Grid Resources: None (standalone wind generation; no storage mentioned)
Description/Notes: Repowered wind farm in the Altamont Pass Wind Resource Area, California, using 38 Mitsubishi MWT-1000A 1 MW turbines for a total installed capacity of 38 MW. Came online in December 2006. Owned by Babcock & Brown Group and GE/GE Energy Financial Services; power supplied to PG&E under California RPS. No energy storage component is described.

Owner(s): Babcock & Brown Group / Babcock & Brown Wind Partners (BBW); General Electric (GE Energy Financial Services); project company: Buena Vista Energy LLC
Funding Source 1: GE Energy Financial Services (Class A/tax equity)

Paired Grid Resources: Nuclear generation; part of the UkraineÃƒÆ’Ã‚Â¢ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¡Ã‚Â¬ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â€šÂ¬Ã…â€œEU Power Bridge for cross-border transmission. Proposed linkage of Unit 2 to Burshtyn Energy Island and Polish transmission lines (pending).
Description/Notes: State-owned EnergoatomÃƒÆ’Ã‚Â¢ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¡Ã‚Â¬ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¾Ã‚Â¢s plant near Netishyn with two operational 1,000 MW units (Unit 1 in 1987; Unit 2 in 2004) and two additional 1,000 MW units (Units 3 and 4) under resumed construction. Construction on Units 3/4 began in 1985/1986, was suspended in 1990 after Chernobyl, and resumed in Nov 2020. Ukraine canceled the prior Russian VVER-1000/V-392B plan in 2017; a 2021 plan proposes Westinghouse AP1000 reactors for Units 3/4 (pending parliamentary approval as of Feb 2022). The site is part of the UkraineÃƒÆ’Ã‚Â¢ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â‚¬Å¡Ã‚Â¬ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â€šÂ¬Ã…â€œEU Power Bridge; potential export via Unit 2 to Burshtyn Energy Island and Poland remains to be finalized. Estimated funding for Units 3/4 is UAH 723.4bn (~$28bn); earlier Russian financing was scrapped in 2017; EBRD support noted; talks with US EXIM ongoing. Key contractors include Westinghouse (AP1000 supply), Penetron (concrete protection), Elbud Katowice (power transmission development), and Eltel Networks (750 kV line and switchyards).
ISO/RTO: NPC Ukrenergo (national TSO; ENTSO-E synchronous area)
Debt Provider: US EXIM Bank (negotiations in progress); prior 2010 Russian Federation loan scrapped in 2017
Funding Source 1: Electricity export revenues from Unit 2 (proposed to fund Units 3 and 4)
Funding Source 2: European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) support

Construction and operations permit received Dec 2025; FID targeted summer 2026; commissioning planned for 2028. Onshore landfall works near Dierhagen began in 2025.

Paired Grid Resources: None (offshore wind generation; grid connection via BorWin Delta converter platform)
Description/Notes: Vattenfallâ€™s two-site offshore wind cluster in the German North Sea totals ~1.61 GW (Nordlicht 1: 980 MW; Nordlicht 2: 630 MW) using 112 Vestas V236-15 MW turbines. BSH granted planning approval for Nordlicht 1 in Jan 2025 and for Nordlicht 2 in Oct 2025. FID for Nordlicht 1 was made in Mar 2025; a conditional FID for Nordlicht 2 was issued pending permitting. Offshore construction for Nordlicht 1 is scheduled to begin in 2026 and for Nordlicht 2 in 2027, with commercial operations expected in 2028. Expected annual output is ~6 TWh, supplying ~1.7 million households. Located ~85â€“96 km north of Borkum in waters ~37â€“39 m deep. Each turbine on 10 m monopiles; ~196 km of inter-array cables; power exported to Amprionâ€™s NOR-6-3 grid connection via BorWin Delta. PPAs include 75 MW with Salzgitter (15 years) and ~450 GWh/yr with LyondellBasell (15 years). BASF divested its prior 49% stake to Vattenfall in Mar 2025 in exchange for long-term renewable supply. Key contractors: EEW SPC (monopiles), CS WIND Offshore (transition pieces), DEME (foundation installation), Jan De Nul (inter-array cabling), Vestas (turbines and service), LD Armateurs (SOV), ArcelorMittal (low-carbon steel).
ISO/RTO: N/A (Germany has no ISO/RTO; TSO model via Amprion)
Installer: DEME (monopile and transition piece installation); Jan De Nul (inter-array cable installation)
O&M Contractor: Vestas (5-year service and warranty + 25-year operations support); LD Armateurs (service operation vessel support)
Owner(s): Vattenfall (100% ownership after BASF divested its prior 49% stake in March 2025)

Project/Plant Name: Abydos Phase II Solar Power Plant (Integrated BESS)
Description/Notes: Abydos Phase II in Aswan Governorate, Egypt, integrates a 600 MWh battery energy storage system (BESS) with a 1 GW solar PV plant. Commercial operations are expected May 2026 under a 25-year PPA with EETC. Financing includes IFC (debt and blended finance) and FMO; EPC by a CEEC-led consortium; owner’s engineer DNV; GameChange Solar Genius Tracker supply; JA Solar modules. The source does not specify the BESS rated power (MW) or discharge duration.
ISO/RTO: N/A – Egypt does not utilize an ISO/RTO; national grid operated by EETC
System Operator: China Energy Engineering Corporation (CEEC) consortium – O&M operator for the first two years
Installer: China Energy Engineering Corporation (CEEC)–led EPC consortium (China Energy International Group, Southwest Electric Power Design Institute, Zhejiang Thermal Power Construction)
Developer: Abydos for Renewable Energy SAE (wholly owned by Amea Power)
O&M Contractor: CEEC-led consortium (operations for first two years)
EPC 3: Southwest Electric Power Design Institute; Zhejiang Thermal Power Construction
Ownership Model (Other): Independent Power Producer (IPP) via SPV wholly owned by AMEA Power
Owner(s): Abydos for Renewable Energy SAE (SPV), wholly owned by AMEA Power
Debt Provider: International Finance Corporation (IFC); B lenders and parallel lenders (incl. FMO with $100m)
Funding Source 2: International Finance Corporation (IFC) - short-term bridge facility
Funding Source 3: B lenders and parallel lenders (including FMO)

Project/Plant Name: O Mon IV Combined Cycle Power Plant (O Mon 4)
Paired Grid Resources: Natural gas supply from Block B gas field via 431km pipeline (Block B – O Mon gas-to-power project)
Description/Notes: 1,155MW CCGT plant developed by Petrovietnam in Can Tho City, Mekong Delta. Ground-breaking occurred in late Aug 2025 with targeted operation by Dec 2028. Plant will use two Mitsubishi M701JAC gas turbines (>64% combined-cycle efficiency, air-cooled) to improve grid reliability and reduce emissions compared to coal. Part of the O Mon Power Centre and the broader Block B – O Mon gas-to-power initiative. EPC by Doosan Enerbility–PECC2 JV (Jun 2025); Mitsubishi Power supplying two M701JAC turbines (Sep 2025); project management consultancy by consortium of Fichtner, EVNPECC4, and Vietnam Institute for Building Science and Technology (Aug 2025).
Street Address: O Mon Power Centre, Phuoc Thoi Ward, O Mon District
ISO/RTO: N/A (Vietnam does not operate an ISO/RTO market structure)
System Operator: National Load Dispatch Centre (A0) under Vietnam Electricity (EVN)
EPC 2: Power Engineering Consulting Joint Stock Company 2 (PECC2)
